does: This paper addresses the weak-to-strong generalization problem in LLM alignment by developing a transfer learning framework that enables training stronger models using feedback from weaker supervisors. The authors prove that weak-to-strong generalization is theoretically possible by extracting latent knowledge from pre-trained models. They formalize the problem as a transfer learning task where a latent concept prior from a weak model is transferred to a stronger pre-trained model. The key technical contribution is showing that naive fine-tuning approaches have fundamental limitations, but an alternative refinement-based method overcomes these constraints. The refinement approach leverages problem structure to preserve capability while incorporating weak supervision, with theoretical guarantees and empirical validation demonstrating practical applicability.
connects: This work extends the long-standing challenge of learning from imperfect or limited feedback, building on decades of research in human-in-the-loop machine learning and learning from weak supervision. It directly responds to concerns in the LLM alignment literature about whether superhuman capabilities can coexist with safety guarantees when trained on human feedback that may be less capable than the model itself. The paper connects to transfer learning theory, particularly work on domain adaptation and knowledge distillation, while drawing on recent advances in mechanistic interpretability and latent knowledge elicitation in neural networks. It also relates to foundational work on supervision and capability-preserving training, addressing practical challenges that have emerged as LLMs scale beyond human expertise in various domains.
enables: This framework opens pathways toward scalable AI alignment that does not require human feedback to match model capabilities, addressing a critical bottleneck in safety practices for advanced AI systems. The refinement-based approach enables future work on aligning increasingly powerful models with limited human oversight, potentially supporting alignment of superhuman or domain-expert systems. The theoretical insights into latent knowledge transfer could inform broader approaches to knowledge elicitation and model steering in pre-trained systems. This work also suggests directions for understanding what information weak models can meaningfully convey to strong models, enabling research into mixed-capability collaborative training scenarios and hierarchical alignment strategies across model capabilities.
